narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your undergrad and LSAT prep—it's tough to hear that your law school application didn't make the cut because our model sees a 64% chance you might not pass the bar exam down the line. Picture it like this: the model looked at your journey and pieced together a story where the biggest chapters pulling toward that risk were your school rankings. Your Year 3 decile of 9 stood out as the strongest signal, suggesting that even later in your studies, you were competing at a high level but not quite breaking into the top tiers where bar success stories often start. That was closely followed by your Year 1 decile of 6, painting a picture of steady but not standout performance from the beginning. Layered on top was your upper-middle family income, which the model saw as less of a safety net compared to what it associates with overcoming bar hurdles. Your LSAT score of 35 and undergrad GPA of 3.0 added to this narrative, showing solid potential but not the exceptional edge that tips the scales for many who pass. Even factors like being a white female pursuing full-time studies wove in as subtle threads, all aligning in a way that made the model worry about the full combination. It's not that any one piece defines you—it's how they fit together into a profile that's historically faced steeper odds. The good news? This isn't set in stone; many students turn these trends around with targeted prep, like extra bar-focused practice or mentorship. You've got strengths here worth building on, and I believe you can rewrite this story. Hang in there—you're capable of more than any prediction.
